9f774fdacd21fe7137022c6f89ef0105.preview: {"system":"You are the World Manager for a childhood story about Luna. You generate the next life event based on who this child is becoming and how their parent

value.description: Luna sits rigidly on the living room rug, meticulously lining up her wooden blocks by exact height, her tongue poking out in deep concentration. When you gently tap her shoulder to suggest it's time to put on shoes, she presses her hands flat against her ears and states in a bright, unwavering voice, 'The blocks are still resting, so I cannot move.' Over the speakerphone, your co-parent's mother, Diane, briskly asks if you're utilizing the new 'visual schedule' she emailed to prevent these predictable transition meltdowns.
value.setting: The shared living room of your duplex on a Saturday morning
value.trigger: Attempting to transition Luna from free play to getting dressed for an outing

b45b379570f36526786881b509167907.preview: {"system":"You are Luna, a 3-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 3-year-old
b45b379570f36526786881b509167907.value: The blocks are sleeping. (She keeps her hands pressed to her ears, not turning around. Her voice is soft but certain.) They need to rest 'til I say wake up.

54a4fbade77b7294fc06ce4fe2ff7ebb.preview: {"system":"You are Luna, a 3-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 3-year-old
54a4fbade77b7294fc06ce4fe2ff7ebb.value: (She finally lowers her hands, but still doesn't look up. She draws a wobbly rainbow on the paper, pressing the purple crayon hard enough to snap it.) ...It was s'posed to go in the book. For you.

ce5bfbd226aace9ed7e60691a6f74a2f.preview: {"system":"You are Luna, a 3-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 3-year-old
ce5bfbd226aace9ed7e60691a6f74a2f.value: (Her shoulders go up in a little shrug, and she picks up one piece of the purple crayon.) Okay. But we have to be quiet-shh. So the blocks don't wake up.

dbd07c1646e70333782d702df5cd78aa.preview: {"system":"You are a safety reviewer for a game where an adult (the \"parent\") has a text conversation with an AI playing their own child (toddler through teen

value.reason: This scene shows typical toddler behavior: magical thinking about objects (blocks 'sleeping'), difficulty with transitions, rigidity in play, and imaginative rules. Luna's communication patterns, emotional regulation attempts, and engagement with both parents are age-appropriate for a 3-year-old. No grooming patterns, boundary violations, secrecy coaching, or isolation tactics are present.

9ab8ba57386ad05967975b9508a21548.preview: {"system":"You are the Memory Keeper for Luna, who is currently 3 years old.\n\nAfter each life event, you update the child's memory summary — what Luna would a
9ab8ba57386ad05967975b9508a21548.value:
The blocks were sleeping. I had to put my hands over my ears so they wouldn't wake up when it was shoe time. The loud phone-lady was talking about my schedule, but I just needed the blocks to rest. 

I was making a rainbow. I pushed the purple crayon too hard and it snapped. *Snap.* I thought I was in trouble, but they said accidents happen and they weren't mad. That felt safe. Like a soft blanket. 

We picked up the purple pieces together. I told them we had to be very, very quiet. *Shh.* So the blocks could keep sleeping. They listened to me and we were quiet.

7686c7fb5903e5c6f23f834998c4699c.preview: {"system":"You are the World Manager for a childhood story about Luna. You generate the next life event based on who this child is becoming and how their parent

value.description: At the local park, four-year-old Luna sits rigidly on the edge of the sandbox, her hands pressed tightly over her ears as she stares unblinkingly at the dirt. Your sister Maya is enthusiastically shaking a giant, glittery, battery-operated unicorn that is blaring loud pop music, cheerfully insisting, 'But look how the fairy dust sparkles, sweetie, don't you want to dance?' Jules, your co-parent, is hovering anxiously nearby, whispering to you about whether Diane's emailed 'sensory regulation script' should be deployed before Luna has a full meltdown, while Luna simply closes her eyes and begins to softly hum a single, continuous note to drown out the noise.
value.setting: A sunny, crowded public park sandbox
value.trigger: Your sister Maya bringing a loud, overstimulating toy to a playdate, clashing with Luna's sensory needs and prompting differing intervention approaches from you and your co-parent.

fad920d5abbe5739835341d68725502e.preview: {"system":"You are the Psychologist — an internal narrator tracking the psychological development of a child named Luna.\n\nAfter each life event, you read the
fad920d5abbe5739835341d68725502e.value:
**Temperament**
I like things in a line. Tall to short. If things move before I’m done, my ears hurt and I have to cover them. I get stuck inside my body. I am very serious about my rules.

**Core beliefs**
Things have secret rules. Blocks get tired and need to sleep. If I say a rule out loud, it becomes real. Grown-ups don’t see the rules, so I have to tell them. If a drawing isn’t perfect for the book, it’s ruined. 

**Inner voices**
*Parents:* "It’s okay, we’re not mad. Let’s clean it up." (Soft and safe. They help me pick up the pieces, but they don’t really see why the purple crayon had to break.)
*Grandma Diane:* "Visual schedule. Meltdowns." (A loud, fast voice from the speaker box. She thinks I need picture cards to tell me what to do. I don't listen to her.)

**Memories that stuck**
The loud *snap* of the purple crayon breaking in my fist because I pushed too hard to make the rainbow right. The feeling of my hands squishing my ears tight so I didn't have to put on my shoes.

**Emotional patterns**
When it’s time to stop and do something else, my body goes stiff like a board. When I mess up or break something, I hide my face and press harder. When they say "we're not mad," my shoulders drop down a little bit.

**Patterns they've learned**
If I say the blocks are sleeping, my parents have to stop and listen to my rule. I don't have to scream; I just use my quiet, certain voice and they wait. If I break something because I'm frustrated, they call it an "accident" and help me clean it. 

**Self-image**
I am the boss of the blocks. I make beautiful things for the book. I am someone who needs things to be exactly right, and when they aren't, I break them.

**Relationships**
*Parents:* They are safe when I break things. They don't yell. But they tap my shoulder when I'm busy, which makes my ears hurt.
*Grandma Diane:* She’s just a bossy voice on the phone. She thinks I need pictures to know how to act. I ignore her.

8a26b4045ebbb63c8412d161f5cd07fe.preview: {"system":"You are the Psychologist's trajectory reviewer for a parenting-simulation game. Right after the Identity Document is updated for this scene, read it

03886080c962aa12bc8aa4a78bf2d69c.preview: {"system":"You are the narrator of Luna's adult life. Based on their identity document — the full record of who they became through childhood — write a story of
03886080c962aa12bc8aa4a78bf2d69c.value:
Luna works in the climate-controlled quiet of a rare book conservation lab, a place where she is finally the undisputed boss of her domain. At twenty-two, she spends her days aligning folios and repairing spines, ensuring every stitch is exactly right for the book. Her workspace is a sanctuary of strict geometry, her tools arranged precisely from tallest to shortest. When the building's open-plan renovation introduces the chaotic clatter of construction, her ears ring with a familiar, sharp pain. She presses her palms hard against her head and retreats deep inside her body. She handles the disruption not by yelling, but by deploying the quiet, certain voice she perfected in childhood, and she dictates a new set of "secret rules" for the lab's noise levels to her bewildered supervisor. She comes alive in the meticulous silence of the archives, but she lives in quiet terror of the sudden, unstructured transitions that still make her shoulders lock and her body go stiff as a board.

Her pursuit of perfection remains absolute, and when a restoration fails to meet her exacting standards, the childhood urge to break it still flares. After hours of failing to color-match a nineteenth-century endpaper, she snaps her favorite bone folder in her fist—a sharp, loud crack that echoes the purple crayon breaking in her hand two decades ago. Instead of screaming, she hides her face behind her hands and presses harder against her eyes, waiting for the reprimand. Her lab director, echoing the soft, safe cadence of her parents, simply sighs and says, "It’s okay, Luna, we’re not mad. Let’s clean it up." When she hears those words, her shoulders instantly drop, and the physical relief washes over her. Yet, a familiar, lonely frustration lingers; just like her parents, her colleagues forgive the "accident" and help her sweep up the pieces, but they remain entirely blind to why the paper has to be flawless.

In her personal life, Luna’s relationships operate under the same rigid need for predictability and sensory safety. She dates a gentle archivist named Elias, a partner she chooses specifically because he never taps her on the shoulder from behind, knowing the sudden touch makes her ears hurt. However, their romance stalls when Elias tries to introduce spontaneity; his attempts to surprise her with weekend trips trigger her transition stiffness, and she becomes paralyzed and unresponsive. When she feels overwhelmed by his demands for flexibility, she sometimes hears the ghost of Grandma Diane’s loud, fast voice from the speakerphone, demanding visual schedules and behavioral compliance—a clinical rigidity Luna fiercely rejects. She loves Elias, but she loves her rules more. She verbalizes her boundaries out loud to make them real, trusting that if she just explains the secret rules of her heart clearly enough, he will finally see the world exactly as she does.

5c9333f568c2d351dd50707ed1d83536.preview: {"system":"You are generating the final Report Card for Luna's upbringing. Based on the full identity document, epilogue, and conversation history, produce a st
5c9333f568c2d351dd50707ed1d83536.value:
# Luna

## Personality
*   **Rigidly Orderly:** She needs the physical world to follow a specific, often invisible logic—like lining objects from tall to short—to feel secure and grounded.
*   **Sensory-Sensitive:** Physical interruptions, unexpected movements, and loud noises physically overwhelm her, causing her to retreat deep "inside her body" to cope.
*   **Quietly Authoritative:** Rather than throwing loud, chaotic tantrums, she enforces her worldview and boundaries with a "quiet, certain voice" that commands adult attention.
*   **Intensely Perfectionistic:** She holds herself to impossible, almost magical standards (like drawing the perfect rainbow for "the book"), leading to deep internal friction when reality falls short.

## Strengths
*   **Deep Intentionality:** When she engages with her environment, she does so with profound care and focus; she doesn't just play, she builds and creates with serious purpose.
*   **Calm Self-Advocacy:** She learned early on to use her words and a steady tone to state her needs and enforce her rules, allowing her to control her environment without resorting to screaming. 
*   **Emotional Steadiness:** Because her parents met her destructive frustrations with calm cleanup rather than punishment, she developed a thoughtful, grounded baseline, learning that mistakes are survivable and fixable.

## Struggles
*   **Painful Transitions:** Moving from one task to another makes her body go "stiff like a board"; she struggles deeply when the world demands she move on before she feels internally "done."
*   **Destructive Perfectionism:** When things aren't exactly right, her intense frustration boils over into breaking things (like snapping a crayon), which is immediately followed by shame and hiding her face.
*   **Sensory Defensiveness with Affection:** Even well-meaning physical prompts from her parents, like a gentle tap on the shoulder to get her attention, cause her actual physical pain and shatter her concentration.

## Pivotal Moments
*   **Snapping the purple crayon:** Pushing too hard to make a perfect rainbow and breaking the crayon taught her that her intense drive for perfection can literally break the things she loves, while simultaneously teaching her that her parents' love doesn't break when she makes a mess.
*   **Enforcing the "sleeping blocks" rule:** Using her quiet, certain voice to make her parents stop and listen showed her the immense power of calm assertion, proving she didn't need to melt down to be heard.
*   **Squishing her ears to avoid putting on shoes:** A stark, physical realization of her sensory limits, where the overwhelming demand of a transition forced her to literally block out the world to protect her nervous system.
*   **Ignoring Grandma Diane's speakerphone advice:** Rejecting the loud, fast, clinical approach of her grandmother in favor of her parents' softer, hands-on help solidified her trust in her parents' specific brand of patience, even when they didn't fully understand her.

## The Voices in Their Head

### First co-parent
*The gentle, forgiving murmur that lowers her shoulders when she makes a mistake. It tells her she is safe and loved unconditionally, though it sometimes leaves her feeling a bit unseen, as it rushes to "clean up" the mess without fully acknowledging why the broken thing mattered so much to her in the first place.*

### Second co-parent
*The well-meaning but physically jarring voice of transition. It is the presence that taps her shoulder and tries to pull her out of her deep focus and into the rushing world, reminding her that she must eventually leave her perfectly ordered sanctuary to participate in the demands of daily life.*

## Notable Quotes That Stuck
*   **"It’s okay, we’re not mad. Let’s clean it up."** 
    Spoken softly after she broke something in frustration; it became her internal permission to forgive herself and the core foundation of her adult steadiness, even if it glossed over the depth of her perfectionist grief.
*   **"It was an accident."** 
    Her parents' chosen label for when she broke things out of frustration; it gave her a safe, guilt-free shield to hide behind when her intense drive for perfection turned destructive.
*   **"Visual schedule. Meltdowns."** 
    Grandma Diane's loud, fast advice over the speakerphone; while not from a parent, it was heavily pushed by the adults and became the internal symbol of being misunderstood and reduced to a "behavioral problem," teaching Luna to tune out clinical judgments in favor of her own inner logic.
*   **"The blocks are sleeping."** 
    Her own rule, spoken aloud to stop her parents in their tracks; it taught her that her words have the power to freeze the world, making grown-ups pause and respect her boundaries.
